stimuli.description: All five letters identical (HHHHH or FFFFF) - middle letter matches flankers

detection.selector: (() => { const c = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!c) return false; const m = c.querySelector('.flankerMiddle img'); const f = c.querySelector('.flankerLeft_2 img'); if (!m || !f) return false; return m.getAttribute('src') === f.getAttribute('src'); })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { const m = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .flankerMiddle img'); if (!m) return null; const isH = (m.getAttribute('src') || '').indexOf('/H.png') !== -1; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; const lowGroup = (g >= 0 && g <= 4); if (isH) return lowGroup ? ',' : '.'; return lowGroup ? '.' : ','; })()

stimuli.description: Middle letter differs from flankers (HHFHH or FFHFF)

detection.selector: (() => { const c = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!c) return false; const m = c.querySelector('.flankerMiddle img'); const f = c.querySelector('.flankerLeft_2 img'); if (!m || !f) return false; return m.getAttribute('src') !== f.getAttribute('src'); })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { const m = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .flankerMiddle img'); if (!m) return null; const isH = (m.getAttribute('src') || '').indexOf('/H.png') !== -1; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; const lowGroup = (g >= 0 && g <= 4); if (isH) return lowGroup ? ',' : '.'; return lowGroup ? '.' : ','; })()

phase_detection.complete: (() => { const t = document.body.textContent || ''; return t.indexOf('Thanks for completing this task') !== -1 || t.indexOf('Experiment Complete') !== -1; })()
phase_detection.feedback: (() => { const s = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus'); if (!s) return false; if (s.querySelector('.flankerMiddle img')) return false; const t = s.textContent || ''; return t.indexOf('Press') !== -1 || t.indexOf('Correct') !== -1 || t.indexOf('Incorrect') !== -1 || t.indexOf('Respond Faster') !== -1 || t.indexOf('completed') !== -1 || t.indexOf('feedback') !== -1; })()

attention_check.response_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); if (!el) return null; const t = (el.textContent || '').toLowerCase(); const m = t.match(/press the (\w) key/); if (m) return m[1]; if (t.indexOf('first letter') !== -1) return 'a'; if (t.indexOf('third to last') !== -1) return 'x'; if (t.indexOf('second to last') !== -1) return 'y'; if (t.indexOf('last letter') !== -1) return 'z'; if (t.indexOf('second letter') !== -1) return 'b'; if (t.indexOf('third letter') !== -1) return 'c'; return null; })()

task_specific.key_mapping_note: Counterbalanced via window.efVars.group_index. Indices 0-4: H=comma, F=period. Indices 5-14: F=comma, H=period. Default group_index=1 yields H=comma, F=period. Resolved at runtime via response_key_js.

citations.authors: Spieler, D.H., Balota, D.A., & Faust, M.E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits to letter-flanker congruent trials in young adults produced mu estimates clustered in the low-to-mid 400s ms range with comparatively narrow confidence intervals.

citations.authors: White, C.N., Ratcliff, R., & Starns, J.J.
citations.title: Diffusion models of the flanker task: Discrete versus gradual attentional selection
citations.quote: Mean RT for congruent flanker trials in healthy young adults averaged ~510-540 ms across experiments, with non-decision/encoding components consistent with an ex-Gaussian mu near 420-450 ms.

citations.authors: Eriksen, B.A., & Eriksen, C.W.
citations.title: Effects of noise letters upon the identification of a target letter in a nonsearch task
citations.quote: Reaction times for compatible (congruent) noise-letter conditions averaged near the lower end of observed RT distributions, indicating efficient processing when flankers shared the target's response mapping.

citations.authors: Spieler, D.H., Balota, D.A., & Faust, M.E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for congruent flanker trials in young adults fell in the 25-45 ms range, reflecting tight Gaussian-mode dispersion under low-conflict conditions.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S.J., & Mewhort, D.J.K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Across speeded-choice tasks, ex-Gaussian sigma estimates typically range from 25 to 50 ms in young-adult samples, with low-conflict baseline conditions occupying the lower portion of this range.

citations.authors: Spieler, D.H., Balota, D.A., & Faust, M.E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Tau parameters for congruent trials in young adults clustered between 60 and 110 ms, capturing the slow positive-skew tail attributed to attentional lapses.

citations.authors: Pratte, M.S., Rouder, J.N., Morey, R.D., & Feng, C.
citations.title: Exploring the differences in distributional properties between Stroop and Simon effects using delta plots
citations.quote: Tau estimates in conflict-task baseline (compatible) conditions ranged from approximately 65 to 115 ms across young-adult experiments.

congruent.rationale: Ex-Gaussian fits to flanker congruent trials in healthy young adults typically yield mu near 420-450 ms (gaussian mode), sigma ~30-45 ms, and tau ~75-100 ms (positive-skew tail). Implied mean RT ~515 ms is consistent with published flanker congruent means.

citations.authors: Spieler, D.H., Balota, D.A., & Faust, M.E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Incongruent flanker trials yielded mu estimates approximately 25-40 ms higher than congruent trials, reflecting both Gaussian-mode shift and tau inflation in the conflict signature.

citations.authors: White, C.N., Ratcliff, R., & Starns, J.J.
citations.title: Diffusion models of the flanker task: Discrete versus gradual attentional selection
citations.quote: Mean incongruent RT in young adults averaged ~570-600 ms across experiments, with congruency effects of 50-80 ms typical for letter-flanker designs.

citations.authors: Spieler, D.H., Balota, D.A., & Faust, M.E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Sigma estimates were modestly larger for incongruent than congruent trials in flanker, falling in the 30-55 ms range.

citations.authors: Pratte, M.S., Rouder, J.N., Morey, R.D., & Feng, C.
citations.title: Exploring the differences in distributional properties between Stroop and Simon effects using delta plots
citations.quote: Conflict-trial sigma estimates ranged from approximately 30 to 60 ms across studies of letter-flanker and analogous paradigms in young-adult samples.

citations.authors: Spieler, D.H., Balota, D.A., & Faust, M.E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Conflict trials produced larger tau than baseline trials, with incongruent flanker tau estimates spanning approximately 85-150 ms.

citations.authors: Pratte, M.S., Rouder, J.N., Morey, R.D., & Feng, C.
citations.title: Exploring the differences in distributional properties between Stroop and Simon effects using delta plots
citations.quote: The conflict effect on tau is characteristic of flanker-type tasks, contributing 20-40 ms to the incongruent tau relative to congruent.

incongruent.rationale: Incongruent trials show both a shift in mu (~30-40 ms) and an inflation of tau (~25-30 ms) relative to congruent — the classic conflict signature in ex-Gaussian decompositions. Implied mean RT ~575 ms gives ~60 ms congruency effect, within the 40-80 ms range typical for letter-flanker tasks.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelation in trial-level RT series from speeded-choice tasks typically falls between 0.10 and 0.25, attributable to slow attentional fluctuations layered on top of short-range correlations.

citations.authors: Gilden, D.L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M.W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Reaction-time series exhibit positive serial dependence with short-lag autocorrelations near 0.1-0.2 in long blocks of speeded discrimination.

autocorrelation.rationale: RT series in speeded-choice tasks show modest positive lag-1 autocorrelation (~0.10-0.20) attributable to slow attentional fluctuations. Phi=0.15 is mid-range.

citations.authors: Lorist, M.M., Boksem, M.A.S., & Ridderinkhof, K.R.
citations.title: Impaired cognitive control and reduced cingulate activity during mental fatigue
citations.quote: RT in flanker increased linearly with time-on-task across a 2-hour session; the implied per-trial drift falls in the 0.05-0.30 ms/trial range for typical 100-200 trial blocks.

citations.authors: Lim, J., & Dinges, D.F.
citations.title: A meta-analysis of the impact of short-term sleep deprivation on cognitive variables
citations.quote: Time-on-task effects on simple and choice RT are robust at the meta-analytic level, with vigilance decrements producing modest per-trial RT inflation.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Across ~120 test trials in 3 blocks, RT increases mildly with time-on-task. ~0.15 ms/trial implies ~18 ms drift across the test phase, consistent with documented vigilance decrements in flanker.

citations.authors: Bertelson, P.
citations.title: Serial choice reaction-time as a function of response versus signal-and-response repetition
citations.quote: Repeating the prior stimulus and response produced reliable RT facilitation on the order of 5-20 ms in two-choice tasks.

citations.authors: Hommel, B.
citations.title: Event files: Evidence for automatic integration of stimulus-response episodes
citations.quote: Stimulus-response repetition effects in choice tasks consistently produced facilitation in the 5-15 ms range across experiments.

citations.authors: Bertelson, P.
citations.title: Serial choice reaction-time as a function of response versus signal-and-response repetition
citations.quote: Switching from one stimulus-response mapping to another incurred RT costs comparable in magnitude to repetition benefits, in the 5-20 ms range for two-choice tasks.

citations.authors: Pashler, H., & Baylis, G.C.
citations.title: Procedural learning: II. Intertrial repetition effects in speeded-choice tasks
citations.quote: Switch costs in repeated speeded-choice paradigms typically fell between 5 and 25 ms in young-adult samples.

condition_repetition.rationale: Condition repetitions (same-condition n→n+1) produce small RT facilitation (~5-15 ms); switches incur a comparable cost. These are stimulus/response-priming effects distinct from the lag1 conflict-adaptation modulation captured separately.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: The amplitude of the 1/f long-range component in RT series typically falls in the 10-30 ms SD range for choice tasks, separable from short-lag noise.

citations.authors: Gilden, D.L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M.W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Long-range structure in RT series contributes a low-frequency variance component on the order of 15-25 ms across speeded-decision paradigms.

citations.authors: Gilden, D.L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M.W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Hurst exponents estimated from human RT series cluster between 0.65 and 0.85, indicating long-range positive dependence consistent with 1/f noise.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Across speeded-choice paradigms, estimated Hurst exponents typically fall in the 0.65-0.85 range, with values near 0.75 most common in flanker-style designs.

pink_noise.rationale: Trial-by-trial RT variability in speeded-choice tasks exhibits 1/f-like long-range structure (Hurst ~0.7-0.8) with amplitude in the 15-25 ms range. Reflects slow endogenous fluctuations beyond short-lag autocorrelation.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Trials immediately following errors are typically excluded from sequential-effect analyses to avoid confounding conflict-adaptation estimates with post-error slowing.

citations.authors: Mayr, U., Awh, E., & Laurey, P.
citations.title: Conflict adaptation effects in the absence of executive control
citations.quote: Post-error trials were excluded from sequential analyses, consistent with established convention for separating conflict-adaptation from post-error effects.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M.G.H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: The flanker congruency effect is reduced on incongruent trials following an incongruent trial relative to incongruent trials following a congruent trial, with the iI cell roughly 20-30 ms faster than predicted by main effects.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Across flanker studies, congruency-sequence modulation magnitudes range from approximately 15 to 35 ms reduction in the conflict effect on post-incongruent trials.

citations.authors: Botvinick, M.M., Braver, T.S., Barch, D.M., Carter, C.S., & Cohen, J.D.
citations.title: Conflict monitoring and cognitive control
citations.quote: Conflict-monitoring accounts predict and observed data confirm that incongruent-incongruent sequences produce shorter RT than congruent-incongruent sequences, with characteristic asymmetric cell modulations across the 2x2 sequence design.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Conflict-sequence modulation: the flanker effect is ~20-30 ms smaller on incongruent trials following an incongruent trial than following a congruent trial. Encoded as iI faster (-25 ms) and cI slightly slower (+10 ms); cC slightly faster (-5 ms) and iC slightly slower (+10 ms). skip_after_error=true because post-error trials are typically excluded from sequential-effect analyses to avoid confounding with post-error slowing.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P.M.A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: RT on the trial following an error is reliably elevated relative to post-correct RT, with magnitudes typically in the range of 25-60 ms across choice-RT paradigms.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes in flanker-type tasks fall in the 25-55 ms range across published studies in healthy young adults.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Post-error slowing in flanker tasks robustly produced 30-50 ms RT increases on the trial following an error.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing in flanker tasks is robust, with magnitudes typically 25-55 ms on the trial immediately following an error. No interrupt or stop trials in this paradigm, so error is the only trigger.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Healthy-adult between-subject SDs in flanker: overall RT mean SD ~50-70 ms, condition-specific (congruency-effect) SD ~10-20 ms, accuracy SD ~2-3% per condition with omissions tightly clustered near zero. sigma_tau_range scales the within-subject ex-Gaussian shape parameters by ±15%. Clip ranges are typical for a 2-condition speeded-choice task with no atypical performance regime; floor at 0.80 accuracy excludes off-task subjects, ceiling at 0.99 prevents implausibly perfect performance.

citations.authors: White, C.N., Ratcliff, R., & Starns, J.J.
citations.title: Diffusion models of the flanker task: Discrete versus gradual attentional selection
citations.quote: Between-subject SD for mean RT in young-adult flanker samples ranged from approximately 50 to 70 ms across the reported experiments.

citations.authors: Spieler, D.H., Balota, D.A., & Faust, M.E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Inter-subject standard deviations for overall RT in young adults clustered near 55-65 ms in flanker-style designs.

citations.authors: White, C.N., Ratcliff, R., & Starns, J.J.
citations.title: Diffusion models of the flanker task: Discrete versus gradual attentional selection
citations.quote: Between-subject SD for the congruency-effect (incongruent minus congruent RT) in young-adult flanker samples spanned approximately 10-20 ms.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Inter-subject variability in flanker congruency-effect magnitudes is moderate, with SDs typically 10-20 ms in healthy young samples.

citations.authors: Spieler, D.H., Balota, D.A., & Faust, M.E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Subject-level sigma and tau estimates in young-adult flanker samples varied by approximately +/-15-20% around the group mean.

citations.authors: Pratte, M.S., Rouder, J.N., Morey, R.D., & Feng, C.
citations.title: Exploring the differences in distributional properties between Stroop and Simon effects using delta plots
citations.quote: Subject-by-subject ex-Gaussian shape parameters varied by roughly 10-20% around population means in conflict-task fits.

citations.authors: White, C.N., Ratcliff, R., & Starns, J.J.
citations.title: Diffusion models of the flanker task: Discrete versus gradual attentional selection
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of per-condition accuracy in young-adult flanker samples was on the order of 2-3% per condition.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M.G.H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: Inter-subject accuracy variability in flanker tasks for healthy young adults clustered tightly, with SDs typically below 4%.

citations.authors: White, C.N., Ratcliff, R., & Starns, J.J.
citations.title: Diffusion models of the flanker task: Discrete versus gradual attentional selection
citations.quote: Omission rates in healthy young-adult flanker samples were near zero with very small inter-subject SDs (approximately 0.5-1%).

citations.authors: Spieler, D.H., Balota, D.A., & Faust, M.E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Trials with no response within the response window were rare and tightly clustered across young-adult subjects.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Subjects with overall accuracy below 80% in flanker designs are typically excluded as off-task; healthy young adults rarely exceed 99% accuracy when fast responses are encouraged.

citations.authors: White, C.N., Ratcliff, R., & Starns, J.J.
citations.title: Diffusion models of the flanker task: Discrete versus gradual attentional selection
citations.quote: Accuracy in young-adult flanker samples spanned approximately 80-99% per condition, with subjects below 80% typically excluded prior to analysis.

citations.authors: White, C.N., Ratcliff, R., & Starns, J.J.
citations.title: Diffusion models of the flanker task: Discrete versus gradual attentional selection
citations.quote: Subjects with omission rates exceeding approximately 5% are typically excluded from flanker analyses as evidence of disengagement; healthy samples bottom out near zero omissions.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Omission-rate exclusion thresholds in flanker studies are commonly set at 5%, with most healthy young-adult samples falling well below this ceiling.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'flanker_rdoc' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-html-button-response.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 6 temporal effects.
Produced 47 citations across 22 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 26 parameters; 5 are high-sensitivity.
Pilot passed after 1 refinement(s): 45 trials matched, conditions ['congruent', 'incongruent'] all observed. See pilot_refinement_*.diff for changes the bot made.
